segments.s219-g145: Literal order: 'From Iran came, they say, Piltan.' از (preposition) = 'from' placed before the place (ایران). بیامد is simple past 'came.' گو is the hearsay particle 'they say' (common in epic/poetic Persian) inserted after the verb to mark that the following names are reported. PIلتن is a proper name/place following the verb as the subject of 'came'.
segments.s219-g146: A simple nominal list following the reported verb: two personal names (فریبرز, کاوس) joined by و 'and', then آن انجمن 'that assembly' (آن = 'that' pointing to a previously known gathering; انجمن = 'assembly/meeting'). The whole phrase completes the reported subject(s) who 'came.'
segments.s219-g147: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past often used for a ruler meaning 'he ordered/commanded.' تا introduces the subordinate clause of result/purpose 'so that / that,' and برکشیدند is past, 3rd-person-plural of برکشیدن 'to lift up/raise' (بر- prefix + کشیدن) — here 'they raised.' کوس is the object 'war-drum.'
segments.s219-g148: This is a compact poetic clause: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from,' گرد here conveys the idea of 'around/the throng of,' سپه = 'the army/host,' کوه = 'mass/heap' used metaphorically for the gathered men, and گشت (past of گشتن) = 'became/turned.' آبنوس = 'ebony' (black) — together: 'from the army's throng the mass became ebony' = they formed a dark mass.
segments.s219-g149: ز = 'from'; کوه = 'mountain'; هماون (a poetic/dialectal form) = 'that same/that very'; برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'; خروش = 'a roar/tumult.' Word order is poetic (location + demonstrative + verb + noun) but straightforward: 'a roar arose from that mountain.'
segments.s219-g150: زمین آمد literally 'the earth came' (here 'the earth heaved/was moved'); آمد is simple past. از بانگ اسپان = 'from the cry/sound of the horses' (از = 'from', بانگ = 'cry/crying out', اسپان = plural 'horses'). بجوش is the present/jussive form of جوشیدن 'to boil/seethe'; used here predicatively to describe the earth ('the earth came, seething') rather than as a direct imperative.
segments.s219-g151: سپهبد = 'spahbed' (military commander). بریشان is the preposition بر + pronoun ایشان 'to/toward them' (literally 'upon them' but here 'to them'). زبان برگشاد is an idiom: 'opened his tongue' = 'began to speak' (زبان = 'speech/tongue' here, برگشاد = 'opened' in past), so the whole clause means 'the spahbed spoke to them.'
segments.s219-g152: ز = 'from/about' (poetic preposition), مازندران = the region Mazandaran; کرد is past 'did/made' used with یاد to mean 'made mention' or 'recounted/remembered'; بسیار = 'many/much' modifying یاد; یاد = 'remembrance/mention.' Together: 'he made many recollections/mentions concerning Mazandaran' = 'he recalled many things about Mazandaran.'
segments.s219-g137: 'ز بس' is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'because of so many' or 'from the abundance of'; it introduces a list of instruments/forces (گرز، شمشیر، پیل، سپاه) joined by و as a simple coordinate series.
segments.s219-g138: میان اندرون = 'between the inner (ranks/quarters)'; باد را uses را as a topical/object marker ('for the wind' / 'even the wind'), and the verb-less clause places نیست (is not) before راه (way) in a slightly inverted, poetic order: 'باد را نیست راه' = 'there is no way for the wind.'
segments.s219-g139: The opening چه is an exclamatory/interrogative 'what/how' introducing a rhetorical question; ایرانیان پیش ما = 'Iranians before us' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of' or 'formerly'), and در چه خاک = 'in what land' (در + چه + خاک), forming a double interrogative phrase.
segments.s219-g140: ز here is causal/poetic 'because of' (introducing the persons who might cause fear); the proper names are coordinated by و, and the final چه باک is another exclamatory question meaning 'what fear / why fear?'.
segments.s219-g141: پراگنده گشتند is a past plural compound meaning 'they became scattered / they scattered' (گشتند from شدن); ازان جایگاه is an ablative phrase 'from that place' modifying the verb.
segments.s219-g142: سوی خیمه خویش = 'toward their own tent(s)'; here خویش is split orthographically into خوی + ش (ش = enclitic possessive 'his/their'), and کردند راه is an idiomatic, somewhat inverted verb phrase 'they made/held a way' = 'they took the road / set out'.
segments.s219-g143: ازان پس = 'after that'; چو here = 'when' (temporal), آگاهی آمد = 'news/information arrived' (آگاهی as 'information' and آمد 'came'), and به طوس = 'to Tous' (dative phrase marking the recipient of the news).
segments.s219-g144: که introduces the reported/result clause 'that ...'; شد is the past of شدن 'became', روی کشور = 'the face/extent of the country', پر = 'full (of)', آوای کوس = 'the sound/cry of the war-drum' — literal: 'that the country's face became full of the drum's sound.'
segments.s219-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' placed before the clause; word order is 'چو + subject (خورشید) + prepositional phrase (بر گنبد لاژورد)' so literally 'when the sun upon the lapis dome...'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' preceding its object گنبد; لاژورد is a postmodifier (color/material) of the dome.
segments.s219-g2: سراپرده‌ای is indefinite (the -ی ending) 'an inner curtain'; زد is the past form of زدن 'struck/drew' used here transitively ('drew/struck a curtain'); ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the material, so 'زد ز دیبای زرد' = 'struck/drew from yellow brocade' (i.e. 'drew a yellow-brocade curtain'). Word order places the verb before the material phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s219-g3: خروشی is a noun 'a cry/roar' with the attributive adjective بلند following it (typical Persian order: noun + adjective). آمد is the simple past/inchoative 'came/rose' and از دیده‌گاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the viewpoint/lookout' indicating source.
segments.s219-g4: بگودرز here functions as the addressee/name being called (vocative use of the name); کای is the explicit vocative particle 'O' (address marker); پهلوان سپاه is a title apposed to the name 'champion/hero of the host'. The sequence is an exclamatory address: 'O Goudarz, O champion of the army.'
segments.s219-g5: سپاه آمد is a subject-verb clause 'the army came/arrived'; و links the clauses. راه نزدیک شد uses نزدیک as a predicate adjective 'near' with شد as the copular/decidual verb 'became/drew' — literally 'the way became near' (i.e. the approach came close).
segments.s219-g6: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'; گرد here means 'dust' (the dust raised by the army), and سپه is the possessor 'of the army' (classical style omits ezāfe); روز تاریک شد is a clause 'the day became dark' with تاریک as predicate and شد marking the change of state.
segments.s219-g7: بجنبید is a classical past narrative form 'he moved/stirred' (the prefixed b‑ is common in classical Persian pasts: بنشست, بگفت, etc.); گودرز is the subject; از جای خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from his place' where خویش is expressed as separate elements (خوی + ش) meaning 'his own'.
segments.s219-g8: بیاورد is the past tense 'he brought' (the same subject carries over). پوینده is used as a substantive/adjectival noun 'the swift-moving one' (i.e. a fast mount/charger); بالای 'above/upon' here means 'to the side/top of' or 'up to' (i.e. brought up to himself); خوی + ش again form the possessive 'his'. The clause therefore means he brought his swift steed up to himself.
segments.s219-g193: چه داری is an interrogative phrase ('what do (you/one) have' or 'what is in...') with داری (present-tense 'have') as the main verb. The noun phrase دژم اختر خویش را is the (definite) object marked by را: دژم (an adjective 'sullen/grim') modifies اختر ('star'), خویش is the possessive formed from خوی + the enclitic ش ('his/her/one's own'), and را signals that this whole phrase is definite/object. Word order here places the interrogative + verb before the object (a common poetic order).
segments.s219-g194: درم بخش is an imperative construction: درم ('dirham') appears before بخش, which here functions as the verb 'give' (bakhshیدن) in the imperative. The conjunction و ('and') links the two gifts; دینار درویش را places the second coin (dinar) with درویش ('the poor/poor person') and the object marker را attached to درویش — in Persian the را on the second object often marks the whole coordinated object phrase as definite/accusative (i.e., 'give a dirham and (give) the dinar to the poor').
segments.s219-g195: بشادی is an adverbial phrase 'with joy' or 'joyfully' that sets the manner. ز introduces the source ('from'), and گردان ایران together describe the source as 'the troop/company of Iran' (گردان = troop, ایران = the Iranians/land/people). گروه here names the company/band (subject) — the phrase orders the adverbial and source before the subject, a typical poetic arrangement.
segments.s219-g196: خروشی (a noun 'a cry/roar' or 'outcry') is the thing that happens, and برآمد is the past tense verb 'arose/issued forth' — together forming the core 'a cry arose'. The prepositional phrase ز بالای کوه ('from the top of the mountain') follows, with ز meaning 'from' and بالای 'atop/above' specifying location (کوه = mountain).
segments.s219-g17: گرفتند is the simple past plural 'they seized/took'. مر is an old/dialectal first‑person form meaning 'to/at me' (a dative/locative clitic), placed before the object phrase to indicate proximity to the speaker. یکدگر را is the definite object 'one another' (یکدگر) marked by را; کنار here functions adverbially 'at the side/nearby'. Word order: verb (گرفتند) + dative/locative clitic (مر) + object with را (یکدگر را) + adjunct (کنار).
segments.s219-g18: خروشی is a noun 'a cry/roar' serving as the subject; برآمد is the past 'rose/issued forth'. ز هر دو is a prepositional phrase 'from both' (ز = 'from', هر = 'each/both'); بزار here is used as a noun 'place'/'position' (derived from 'put/place'), so ز هر دو بزار = 'from both places/groups'. The clause thus states that a roar arose from both places/groups.
segments.s219-g19: فریبرز is the speaker's name (proper noun) followed by گفت, the past tense verb 'said' introducing direct speech. ای is the vocative particle 'O!' placed before a title; سپهدار پیر is the addressee: سپهدار 'commander' and پیر 'old/elder' used adjectivally (a respectful epithet). The sequence is Name + گفت + vocative phrase.
segments.s219-g20: همیشه 'always' is an adverb modifying the following verb. بجنگ here is a jussive/subjunctive/imperative form of 'to fight' ('must fight' / 'fight'), used elliptically to express obligation or command. اندری (اندر + ی) means 'in it'/'therein' (inside/within that place), and ناگزیر is an adjective/adverb 'inevitable' or 'compelled' — together the clause expresses necessity: 'you must always fight there; it is inevitable.' Word order places the adverb first, then the verb phrase, then the locative.
segments.s219-g21: ز here means 'because of' (a poetic/prepositional use). کین 'vengeance/hatred' takes the following name سیاوش as its possessor or source ('the hatred of Siavash'). تو داری زیان is 'you have loss' — تو 'you' (subject), داری present 'have' (possess), and زیان 'loss/damage'. The whole phrase means 'Because of Siavash's hatred you have loss.'
segments.s219-g22: دریغا is an interjection 'Alas!' expressing regret. سواران گودرزیان is a nominal phrase 'the Gudarz horsemen' (سواران 'riders/horsemen', گودرزیان 'the followers/men of Gudarz'); it follows the interjection to comment on who suffers or is involved. The line after the lament names the group in question.
segments.s219-g23: ازیشان ترا is a compressed phrasing: ازیشان = از ایشان 'from them', ترا = ت + را ('to you' with second‑person clitic ت plus object marker را), so the beneficiary is 'you' receiving something from 'them'. مزد بسیار باد is an optative wish: مزد 'reward', بسیار 'great/very much', and باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical Persian). Together: 'May a great reward be (given) to you from them.'
segments.s219-g24: سر بخت here functions as 'the fortune/lot' (بخت = fate; سر in compounds can mark 'head/centre' but is part of the nominal phrase 'سر بخت' = 'the fate/fortune'). دشمن 'enemy' is the possessor/target of that fate. نگونسار is an adjective meaning 'woeful/ill‑fated/tragic', and باد again is the optative 'may'. The clause is a wish: 'May the enemy's fortune be woeful.'
segments.s219-g89: یکی بار (literal 'one time') is an idiomatic temporal phrase meaning 'once' or 'one occasion'. دست من اندر نبرد is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'my hand in the battle' (دست = 'hand' here, اندر = 'in/within', نبرد = 'battle'); together the segment requests a single opportunity to engage (i.e. 'put my hand into the fight once').
segments.s219-g90: نگه کن is an imperative formed from نگه (here 'watch/keep (watch)') + کن (imperative 'do'), so 'keep/watch'. که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'), and برخیزد is a present-subjunctive/optative sense 'may rise/get up' (third person singular). از دشت گرد is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain dust' where گرد is used as 'dust' — so the clause means 'watch that dust may rise from the plain.'
segments.s219-g91: بدانی is a jussive/subjunctive form meaning 'know' / 'you should know' (used as an exhortation). که is the complementizer 'that' / 'who', followed by اندر جهان 'in the world' (prepositional phrase). مرد کیست inverts the normal order for emphasis: مرد (here 'a valiant man/hero') + کی (interrogative 'who') + ست (copula 'is') yields 'who in the world is a (true) man.'
segments.s219-g92: This is a coordination continuing the exhortation: دلیران کدامند ('which ones are the brave') uses کدامند (interrogative 'which are they?') with the plural predicate. و links the clauses, and پیکار چیست is a final copular question 'and what is fighting/battle' (چی = 'what?', ست = 'is'). The sentence enumerates knowledge to be had: who is a man, who are the brave, and what battle is.
segments.s219-g93: بدو is an indirect-object/prepositional clitic meaning 'to him/unto him'. گفت here functions as 'said' (reported speech) despite the lexical sense lists; پیران is the subject 'the elders'. کانوشه بدی is the accusative phrase 'the casket (کانوشه) of evil (بدی)' — the elders are addressing him about that object.
segments.s219-g94: همیشه ز تو دور دست بدی is a comment/assessment: همیشه 'always'; ز تو 'from you' (poetic variant of از); دور دست بدی literally 'the hand/power of evil is far' — idiomatically 'evil's hand/reach has always been far from you' or 'you have always kept evil at a distance.' Note that دست here functions metaphorically as 'reach/power.'
segments.s219-g95: بپیران is a prepositional phrase 'to the elders' (بـ + پیران). چنین 'thus/so' is a sentence adverb 'in this way', and گفت is the verb 'said/spoke' (the khagan is the agent here). خاقان چین is the nominative phrase 'the Khaghan of China' and appears after the verb for poetic inversion: 'to the elders thus spoke the Khaghan of China.'
segments.s219-g96: This is a reported-content clause introduced by که ('that'). کاموس را is the definite object (را marks the object); راه دادی is 'you gave a way/path' (راه = 'path', دادی = past of 'to give'). بکین is an archaic/poetic infinitive/verb meaning 'to take vengeance' (here describing the result: 'you gave Kamus a way to take revenge'). The whole clause reports what the khaghan told the elders.
segments.s219-g161: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' placed at the clause's start for emphasis. بران گونه is a small phrase 'بر آن گونه' where بران (on/at that) + گونه (manner) yields 'in that manner'; رزمی (martial) modifies the action, and کنیم is first-person-plural present-subjunctive/imperative 'let us do' (proposal/ hortative: 'let us fight'). Word order is typical poetic Persian: adverb → locative phrase → manner adjective → verb.
segments.s219-g162: که here introduces a purpose clause ('so that'). این ننگ is the direct object 'this shame'; از is the preposition 'from/of' marking provenance ('from the Iranians'); ایرانیان is the noun 'Iranians'. بفگنیم is an archaic/poetic 1st-person-plural verb form (prefix ب- + verb stem), functioning as subjunctive/optative meaning 'we may blot out / remove' — the whole clause gives the purpose of the fighting.
segments.s219-g163: درفش (standard/banner) is the head noun; سرافراز (lofty/honored) is a postpositive adjective modifying it; خاقان (khagan) functions as the possessor (understood as درفشِ سرافرازِ خاقان). The conjunction و then adds تاج ('and the crown'). This is a coordinated noun list with poetic, compact possession.
segments.s219-g164: سپرهای زرین is a noun + adjective sequence 'shields (plural) — golden' (adjective after noun, common in classical/persian poetic order). و coordinates to the next phrase; آن تخت عاج places the demonstrative آن before تخت ('that throne') with عاج ('ivory') as a postpositive adjective — 'that ivory throne.'
segments.s219-g165: همان gives emphasis/definiteness ('that same'); افسر here means 'helmet' (an older/poetic sense). پیلبانان ('elephant-masters/mahouts') is the possessor of the helmet; بزر (postposed) is an adjective 'great' modifying پیلبانان. The phrase therefore reads 'that very helmet of the great elephant-masters.'
segments.s219-g166: سنانهای زرین is 'the spears (plural) — golden' (spear + adjective after it). و links to زرین کمر, where زرین modifies کمر; کمر here means 'girdle/belt.' The repetition of زرین is poetic parallelism (both items described as golden).
segments.s219-g167: همان 'that same' emphasizes the items; زنگ زرین is 'golden bell' (زنگ as 'bell') and the conjunction و links to زرین جرس 'golden gong/bell' — two near-synonymous nouns, each qualified by زرین. Poetic repetition highlights their preciousness.
segments.s219-g168: که introduces a relative clause 'which.' اندر جهان = 'in the world' (اندر = in). آن refers back to the previously named items ('those'); ندیدست is an archaic negative perfect/experiential form 'has not been seen' (ن- negation + دیدست), and کس in this negative context means 'no one.' So the clause says 'which no one in the world has seen.'
segments.s219-g113: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic position before the subject). The three names follow in a simple coordinated list with و as the copulative 'and'. The sequence places the temporal conjunction before the subject cluster, a common epic construction.
segments.s219-g114: This is a descriptive appositive: بزرگان و شیران = 'the great ones and the lions' (i.e. chiefs and brave men). روز نبرد is a poetically compact genitive: 'the day of battle' (word order is noun+noun without an explicit ezāfe, typical of epic diction). The whole segment qualifies the names in the preceding clause.
segments.s219-g115: بگفتند is the narrative/reporting verb 'they said'. کامد is an archaic present/near-future form 'comes/is coming' used in reported speech; it follows the reporting verb without a modern complementizer. ز ایران سپاه is a poetic word order meaning 'an army from Iran' (preposition + origin + noun).
segments.s219-g116: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a single (group or man)'. پیش رو functions as a compound indicating position/motion 'in front / going before' (پیش + رو); together they mean 'one leading/going ahead'. با درفشی سیاه is a prepositional phrase 'with a black banner', with adjective after noun as usual in Persian.
segments.s219-g117: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کارآگهان (plural of کارآگه) means 'the experienced/knowing ones' here acting as the source group. نامداری is a noun 'renown' used attributively ('renowned'); دمان is treated here as the following noun (likely a name or locality). The phrase is compact and typical of epic diction where descriptive nouns stack without linking particles.
segments.s219-g118: برفت is an archaic/perhaps dialectal past-form meaning 'he/she/it went' (the provided sense list is mismatched, so -1 is given for sense). The clause has a paired motion verb construction برفت و بیامد 'he went and came', with بیامد 'came' in ordinary past. هم is an adverb 'also/even', and اندر زمان is the locative 'in the same time' (اندر is poetic 'in/within'). The whole phrase describes quick or repeated motion in that moment.
segments.s219-g119: This fronted sequence names persons and then gives the reporting verb: فریبرز and کاوس are personal names placed before the report. گفتند is the reporting verb 'they said' but the provided senses list did not include that meaning (hence -1). هست is the copula 'there is / is'. The construction is an epic-style topicalization where names precede the reporting formula.
segments.s219-g120: سپاهی is 'army/host' used as the predicate noun. سرافراز is an attributive adjective 'proud/triumphant/lofty' following the noun. و links the next descriptive term, خسروپرست, which is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'king-worshipping / devoted to the king' (used here as a negative/positive characterization depending on context). The segment is a nominal predicate introduced by هست in the previous segment.
segments.s219-g129: چنین گفت پیران که is a reported-speech frame: 'چنین گفت پیران' = 'thus said the elders' (verb after adverbial for poetic effect). The conjunction/relativizer 'که' introduces the content clause that follows. 'از تخت و گاه' is a prepositional phrase (از + nouns) marking the domain of the speaker's weariness ('of the throne and the court/occasion').
segments.s219-g130: This clause contains two predications about the speaker's state. 'شدم' is the past of شدن 'become' ('I became'); 'سیر' is a predicative adjective 'sated, weary'. The conjunction 'و' links the second predicate 'بیزارم' (بیزار + م = 'I am disgusted/weary of'), where the 1st-person suffix -م marks the experiencer. 'از' introduces the objects of disgust 'هور و ماه' (pomp/splendour and the moon/beautiful ones).
segments.s219-g131: 'که چون من شنیدم' is a temporal/subordinate clause 'when I heard that/when I learned'. Here 'چون' functions as 'when/as' and 'شنیدم' is perfect/past 'I heard/learned'. 'کز' (= که + از) introduces origin 'from Iran' attaching to 'سپاه' (an army), so word order is 'کز ایران سپاه' = 'an army from Iran'.
segments.s219-g132: Two verbs describe the army's motion: 'خرامید' (past) 'strode/proudly advanced' and 'آمد' 'came'. The prepositional prefix 'ب' (ب + دین as written) marks direction 'to/into', so 'بدین رزمگاه' = 'to this battlefield'. The verbs share the subject 'سپاه' from the previous segment.
segments.s219-g133: 'بشد' here means 'it became / it happened' and introduces the change of state. The coordinated subject 'جان و مغز سرم' = 'my life and the marrow of my head' (م suffix on سر = 'my head'). 'پر ز درد' is a predicative phrase 'full of pain' where 'پر' = 'full (of)' and 'ز' = 'of/from'.
segments.s219-g134: 'برآمد' = 'arose/issued forth'. 'یکی' is an indefinite 'a certain / one' introducing the thing that rose. 'از دلم' = 'from my heart' where the clitic -م on 'دل' marks first person possession. 'باد سرد' is the noun phrase 'a cold wind' (باد = wind, سرد = cold) and is the thing that rose.
segments.s219-g135: 'بدو گفت کلباد' is inverted order: 'to him Kalbad said' = 'Kalbad said to him'. The question 'کین درد چیست' is an interrogative: 'چیست' is the copula 'what is', and 'کین' here functions as an archaic/demonstrative 'this' (or intensifier) modifying 'درد' = 'pain'.
segments.s219-g136: 'چرا باید از طوس و رستم گریست' is a rhetorical question formed with the modal 'باید' ('ought/must') plus the verbal noun/infinitive-like form 'گریست' ('to weep' / 'one should weep'). 'از' marks the object of mourning 'طوس و رستم' = 'Tūs and Rostam' (proper names). The construction asks 'why ought one to weep for Tūs and Rostam?'
segments.s219-g121: چو is the classical subordinating particle 'when/if'. رستم is the proper name. نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive third-person form of 'to be' (classical -د ending); here it yields 'is not (present)'. ازو is the enclitic classical form of 'از او' meaning 'from him' (used after the verb/clause). باک نیست is a verbless predication where باک (fear) is negated by نیست 'is not' — literally 'there is no fear from him.'
segments.s219-g122: دم او is a possessive phrase 'his breath' with the pronoun after the noun (archaic/classical ordering). برین is a fused demonstrative/adjectival element ('such/this' in classical Persian, here 'such a/that kind of') formed from بر + ین; together with زهر it means 'that sort of poison'. تریاک 'opium' follows in a coordinating noun phrase; نیست negates the whole nominal predicate — 'his breath is not such a poison nor opium.'
segments.s219-g123: ابا here works as a classical conjunction/verb of refusal ('to refuse' / 'inasmuch as he refused'); آنک introduces a subordinate content clause ('that'). کاموس is used as a proper name in context. روز نبرد is a genitive/compound noun 'the day of battle' (noun + noun). Together this gives 'because he refused that it be the day of battle (or: because Kamus refused the battle-day)'.
segments.s219-g124: همی is the continuous/emphatic particle (classical) that often precedes verbs to show ongoing or emphatic action ('will/does ...'). پیلتن is a proper name/place; را is the definite/object marker (here marks the person affected). ندارد is the present of داشتن used causatively here ('does not let/have [someone] do something' = 'will not allow'). بمرد is the subjunctive/optative form composed of the prefix ب- + مرد (die), here expressing purpose/subordinate clause 'that he die' or infinitival result: 'he will not let Piltan die.'
segments.s219-g125: مبادا is a classical jussive/negative wishing particle meaning 'may it not be' / 'God forbid'. که introduces the subordinate clause. او آید is a third-person present/subjunctive 'that he come'. ایدر is the archaic form of 'اینجا' ('here'). بجنگ is the infinitive/oblique of 'fight' (classical usage after آید gives 'come here to fight'). The clause therefore reads 'May it not be that he come here to fight.'
segments.s219-g126: وگر is a classical conditional particle 'if' (often 'but if' / 'and if'). چند in this classical context functions as an adverb meaning 'even if' / 'though' (an idiomatic use). کاموس here takes the sense 'ocean' (classical Persian word for the sea), and گردد is the present/subjunctive of گردیدن 'to become'. نهنگ is 'whale' (here figuratively 'a great sea-monster' or gigantic thing). The clause is a hypothetical: 'and if even the ocean were to become a whale...' (i.e., even if things grew monstrous).
segments.s219-g127: نه ... نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. The first disjunct is رستم (Rostam). The second disjunct is expressed as از سیستان لشکرست: از سیستان ('from Sistan') modifies لشکر (army/host), and لشکرست is a fused classical form equivalent to 'لشکر است' ('there is an army'). So: 'Neither Rostam nor an army from Sistan (is present).'
segments.s219-g128: فریبرز را uses را in a dative/benefactive sense here: 'for Fereborz' or 'as for Fereborz'. خاک و خون is a coordinated noun phrase 'dust/earth and blood' (i.e., carnage). ایدرست is an archaic fused form (here functioning adverbially/affirmatively: 'here is' / 'indeed (for him)') — used to assert the existence/condition for Fereborz: 'for Fereborz there is dust and blood here.'
segments.s219-g57: بباید is an impersonal modal in classical Persian meaning 'it is necessary' / 'one must'. بدان here is the locative contraction (به + آن) = 'in/at that' (poetic/archaic), placing the action at 'that' battlefield. رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield' and آرمید is a past-form verb 'rested/lay down' used here in the sense 'to rest (for the time being)', completing the main clause.
segments.s219-g58: یکی functions as an indefinite 'one/someone' (here marking an indefinite person or simply 'one'); تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until'. درفش من is a possessive phrase 'my banner/standard', and آید پدید is a compound verbal idea in which آید (present/subjunctive/future sense 'comes') + پدید (appear) together mean 'appears/emerges'. The تا-clause gives the duration/limit for the action in the previous clause.
segments.s219-g59: برفت is a past-tense verbal form (بر + رفتن) meaning 'set off' / 'departed'; the line repeats the verb for stylistic emphasis. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'; و is the coordinating 'and'; گودرز is the proper name 'Goudarz'; با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him' (با + object). The clause order is Subject–Predicate with the repeated verb marking parallel actions.
segments.s219-g60: براه is an archaic/prepositional phrase (به راه) meaning 'on the road' or 'en route'. هماون here functions as a demonstrative/proper-name-like element ('that one' / Homāun) serving as the subject. خرامید is the past tense 'he strode/ambled proudly' (خرامیدن). The final تفت is not used here as the cooking sense; it reads as an isolated name or exclamatory particle in the verse and does not match the dictionary senses given.
segments.s219-g61: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. لشکر is the subject 'army/host'. پدید آمد is a two-word verbal phrase 'appeared / came into view' (پدید = appear, آمد = came). از دیده‌گاه literally 'from the field of sight' = 'into view / on the horizon' — the prepositional phrase specifies where the army appeared.
segments.s219-g62: بشد is an archaic/past verbal form meaning 'it became / there was' (introducing the occurrence). دیده‌بان is the predicate noun 'a lookout / sentinel'. پیش + توران سپاه places that sentinel 'before/in front of the Turanian army' (پیش = before/in front of, توران سپاه = 'Turanian host'). The clause literally reads 'there became a lookout before the Turanian host' = 'a lookout moved forward.'
segments.s219-g63: کز is an archaic preposition/relative meaning 'from' here (contraction of که + از in classical usage) and introduces the origin 'from Iran'. یکی لشکر is 'one army' (یکی = one/a single), آمد is the past 'came', and بدشت is the locative 'to the plain / onto the plain' (بدشت = به دشت). The phrase gives origin + movement: 'from Iran a single army came onto the plain.'
segments.s219-g64: ازان روی is an idiomatic classical phrase meaning 'from that side / on that account' (ازان = 'from that', روی = 'side/face' used adverbially). سوی is the directional preposition 'toward', هماون points to the addressee/target (here 'Homāun' or 'that one'), and گذشت is the past 'passed / moved on'. Together: 'from that side it moved/passed toward Homāun.'
segments.s219-g73: تو دانی چه کردی بدین پنج ماه is a rhetorical question: تو (you) + دانی (present 2sg 'you know') introduces the subordinate interrogative چه کردی ('what you did', with چه as the question word and کردی, past 2nd-singular). بدین پنج ماه is a temporal phrase: بدین = به + این ('to/for these' or 'in these') and پنج ماه = 'five months', so together 'during/for these five months.'
segments.s219-g74: برین دشت با خوار مایه سپاه is a prepositional phrase giving the place and the force: برین = بر + این ('upon/on that'), دشت = 'plain'. با = 'with', and خوار مایه سپاه is a nominal group in which خوار ('despised, lowly') modifies مایه ('resource/contingent') and سپاه ('army') specifies what the m�یه is — 'a paltry/mean contingent of troops.'
segments.s219-g75: کنون ('now') sets the time. چون here functions as a comparative/introducing particle ('as' / 'that'), زمین ('the land') is the topic, سربسر ('throughout, from head to foot') is an adverbial intensifier, and لشکرست is a poetic fused predicate: لشکر + است → 'is a host/army.' Word order is topical (time/topic → comment).
segments.s219-g76: چو introduces a simile ('as/like'). خاقان و منشور is a coordinated noun phrase ('the khaghan and the charter/manifesto'). کنداورست is a poetic compound/predicate (کنداور + است) meaning literally 'is an excavator' or 'one who turns up (digs)'; here it functions figuratively and forms the predicate linked to the preceding چون ('as ... are like').
segments.s219-g77: بمان is the 2nd-singular imperative 'stay/remain'. تا here has the purposive/optative sense 'so that/in order that', and هنرها ('the arts/skills') is the object. پدید آوریم is a 1st-plural subjunctive/optative compound (پدید + آوریم) meaning 'we may cause/bring forth (to appear)'. The verb form آوریم is subjunctive/intentional rather than a bare past.
segments.s219-g78: تو در بستی is literal: تو 'you' + در 'door' (noun) + بستی (2nd-singular past or resultative of بستن 'to close') — 'you shut/keep shut the door.' و is the conjunction 'and'. ما (we) + کلید آوریم: کلید 'key' and آوریم again in the subjunctive/intentional sense 'we will/shall bring' (here physical bringing). The clause pairs your action (closing) with our bringing the key.
segments.s219-g79: گر 'if' introduces a hypothetical clause. از + a series of proper names (کابل و زابل و مای و هند) means 'from Kabul and Zabul and Mây and India' — a list of source regions. Note: مای here appears as a place-name in the poem; the provided lexical senses ('wine' / 'we') do not match that usage, so it is treated as a toponym in context.
segments.s219-g80: شود is the 3rd-singular of شدن 'become' (indeed used here to mean 'it comes about / the face becomes'). روی گیتی = 'the face/appearance of the world' (روی 'face' modifying گیتی 'world'). چو introduces a simile 'as/like', رومی 'Roman/Byzantine', and پرند here is a poetic nominal (in the poem used as 'winged ones' / 'birds' or figuratively 'those who fly'); it's functioning as the term of the comparison. The clause overall is conditional: 'If ... from X the world's face becomes like Romans (winged/that of Romans)'.
segments.s219-g169: همان (demonstrative) = “that/the same,” placed before the noun for emphasis; چتر is the head noun “canopy/cover.” کز is the archaic fused relative/prepositional particle (که + از) meaning “which/from which,” introducing the source phrase دم طاوس نر (“the tail of the male peacock”), where دم here means the ‘tip/tail plume.’ Word order is head noun + relative phrase (typical Persian poetic inversion).
segments.s219-g170: برو here functions as a prepositional/locative element (classical contraction of بر + او = “on it” / “upon it”), marking where the action takes place; بافتستند is an archaic past 3rd‑pl form of بافتن “to weave/embroider” (they wove); چندان modifies گهر as a quantifier “so many/much”; گهر is the direct object “gems/jewels.” The verb at the end agrees with a plural subject implied by the craftsmen/people.
segments.s219-g171: جزین نیز is a conjunctive phrase: جزین “apart from this/except this” + نیز “also/too” = “besides this.” چندی here functions as a small-quantity marker (“a few/some”) and modifies بچنگ آوریم, where بچنگ (literally “into the grasp”) combines with آوریم (present-subjunctive/optative plural “let us bring/capture”) to form the verb phrase “let us seize/capture.” The verb آوریم is in the present-subjunctive/optative sense (proposal ‘let us…’).
segments.s219-g172: چو is a conditional/temporal conjunction here = “when/if.” جان را uses را as the definite object marker (marking جان as the object of the following verb). بکوشیم is present-subjunctive/optative of کوشیدن “to strive/attempt” meaning here “if/we should risk/put our lives to the test.” و links the clauses, and جنگ آوریم repeats the verb آوریم (same form as above) with جنگ “battle/fight” as its object, meaning “we will bring about/engage in battle.”
segments.s219-g173: بلشکر is an adverbial/instrumental phrase “with a host/in force,” placed before the verb for emphasis. چنین گفت is the common poetic formula “thus said”; گفت is the past verb “said” (here used with classical word order ‘adverbial + thus said + speaker’). بیدار is an epithet attached to طوس (Tūs), meaning “awake/alert.” The sequence names the speaker: “Tūs the Awake/Alert.”
segments.s219-g174: که introduces the content clause meaning “that.” هم ... و هم is the correlative pair “both ... and ...” with با + هراسیم and با + فسوس indicating the two predicates. هراسیم is 1st‑pl present “we are frightened/afraid” (classical present/stative), and فسوس is here a noun meaning “insidious whispering/secretive seditious talk.” The whole clause reports Tūs’s statement: “that we are both afraid and beset by whispers.”
segments.s219-g175: همه is the determiner “all/the whole” modifying دامن کوه “the mountain’s skirt/slopes” (دامن کوه = foothills/slope). پر is an adjectival/participle “full (of)” that combines with لشکرست (a contracted copula phrase: لشکر است) to yield “is full of an army” — a typical poetic contraction of است into ست. Word order places the locative phrase before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s219-g176: سر نامداران is a genitive phrase ‘the heads/chiefs of the renowned’ (سر here as ‘chief/top/head’). ببند is the verb root meaning “bind/fasten” (imperative/or participial sense in older usage), and اندرست is a fused classical copula phrase meaning “is within/inside.” Together the clause says literally that the heads (leaders/heads) of the famed are bound/tied up inside (i.e., kept within).
segments.s219-g65: سپهبد (subject) + بشد (past/inchoative verb) + پیش + خاقان چین forms a clause 'the spahbed appeared/advanced before the Khagan of China.' بشد is an older past/inchoative sense 'came/appeared'; پیش here means 'in the presence of' and خاقان چین is a noun phrase 'Khagan of China' (خاقان modified by چین). Word order is Subject – Verb – prepositional phrase.
segments.s219-g66: که introduces a subordinate (reporting/reason) clause: 'that/for an army came from the land of Iran.' آمد is simple past 'came,' سپاهی is the noun 'army/soldiers,' and ز ایران‌زمین is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the land of Iran.' The clause explains why the spahbed approached.
segments.s219-g67: ندانیم is a first‑person plural negative present/gnomic 'we do not know' (ن- prefix + دانیم). چندست is an interrogative nominal 'how many (it is)', and the coordinated clause و سالار کیست asks 'and who is the commander' (کی before the copula ست is the interrogative 'who'). The clauses are coordinated questions about number and leader.
segments.s219-g68: چه سازیم uses چه 'what' with سازیم (first‑person plural subjunctive/optative 'shall we do' / 'we should do') to ask for action. The second coordinate درمان این کار چیست is a nominal question 'what is the remedy/treatment for this affair' with درمان 'remedy' placed before the thing treated (این کار) and چی + ست forming the interrogative copula 'what is'.
segments.s219-g69: بدو is the prepositional/indirect object 'to him' (be + 3rd‑person clitic), گفت is the past 'said' (verb) and کاموس is the speaker's name (subject). رزم آزمای is direct speech or an imperative phrase: رزم 'battle' + آزمای (imperative of آزما) 'try/test' — i.e. 'try the battle' or 'put your strength to the test.' Word order places the indirect object before the verb, then the speaker and the quoted imperative.
segments.s219-g70: بجایی که is a locative/relative 'to a place where' (بـ + جایی), که introduces the relative clause 'where مهتر تو باشی' — مهتر 'chief' with تو باشی (2nd‑person present/subjunctive or jussive) 'you be/shall be.' بپای is a jussive/imperative urging 'stand firm / hold (the place).' The sequence gives an instruction: go to a place where you will be foremost and hold it.
segments.s219-g71: A nominal genitive phrase: بزرگان (the great ones/nobles) + درگاه افراسیاب 'the court of Afrasiab' (درگاه = royal court; افراسیاب is the ruler's name). Together they mean 'the nobles of Afrasiab's court.' Word order is possessee (بزرگان) followed by possessor phrase (درگاه افراسیاب).
segments.s219-g72: سپاهی (an army/troops) modified by بکردار (an adjective 'well‑behaved/ordered') and followed by the metaphorical simile دریای آب ('a sea of water') — i.e. troops so numerous or flowing like a sea. The phrase is a condensed poetic apposition: 'an army, well‑ordered, like a sea of water.'
segments.s219-g9: سوی ... بنهاد روی is a classical Persian sequence meaning 'turned/took his face toward ...'. سوی is a prepositional/directional marker 'toward'. گرد تاریک is a noun + adjective ('dust/cloud' + 'dark') and is the goal introduced by سوی. بنهاد is a past tense verb ('he set/placed' poetically used as 'he turned'), and روی is the direct object meaning 'face'. Word order here is prepositional phrase (سوی + noun), then verb, then the object (روی).
segments.s219-g10: همی شد is a simple past/old epic imperfective sense ('he became' / 'he was made'), with همی adding a continuous/ongoing poetic flavor. خلیده دل is an adjective phrase where خلیده ('withdrawn,secluded') modifies دل ('heart'), i.e. 'his heart withdrawn'. The coordinate و + راه‌جوی links a second predicative nominal: راه‌جوی ('seeker/traveller') stands with خلیده دل as what he became.
segments.s219-g11: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. نزدیک ایشان رسید is a small clause where نزدیک ('near') modifies the following pronoun ایشان ('them'), and رسید ('arrived/reached') is the verb meaning 'drew near / reached them'. The sequence gives 'he came; when he came near them...'.
segments.s219-g12: This is a canonical Persian SOV clause: the object noun phrase 'درفش فریبرز کاوس' (the banner/standard of Fereborz (of) Kavus) precedes the verb دید ('saw'). The names follow the head noun without additional particles, so the sequence denotes possession/apposition: 'the standard associated with Fereborz (of) Kavus', then the verb دید completes the clause.
segments.s219-g13: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'). او is the clause subject 'he'. بد بایران سپه پیش‌رو is a compact noun phrase functioning as the object of پسندیده in the next segment: بد ('bad/ill-suited') qualifies بایران ('the barren/plain'), and سپه پیش‌رو ('army vanguard/forefront') names the role — together: 'an ill-suited vanguard for the barren plain'. In this poetic economy adjectives and modifiers cluster before the head or in series.
segments.s219-g14: پسندیده is a past participle used as the verbal predicate 'had chosen/approved'. The conjunction و links the following nominal phrase. خوی + ش here form خویش (an archaic split of the reflexive/possessive 'his own'), and سالار نو is a predicate nominal 'new commander'. Together this phrase indicates he appointed/was made himself 'the new commander' (poetic order: reflexive clitic separated).
segments.s219-g15: پیاده شد is a compound verbal expression 'he dismounted / got off (became on-foot)'; شد is past tense auxiliary. از اسپ is a prepositional phrase 'from (his) horse/steed' indicating source. گودرز پیر is an appositive: the proper name گودرز followed by the epithet پیر ('the elder/old'), together identifying the person who dismounted.
segments.s219-g16: A string of epithets follows: همان ('that same/that very') points back demonstratively, لشکر افروز is a compound epithet literally 'army-brightening' (afroz used adjectivally here), and دانش‌پذیر is another epithet 'one who takes/receives knowledge' (a pupil or receptive to learning). Such piled epithets are common in epic Persian to describe a hero in few words.
segments.s219-g97: بکردار is an epithet/adjective used substantively here ('the virtuous one') and functions as the sentence's subject. پیش آورَد is a two-part expression: پیش (an adverb 'before / in the presence of') + آورد (past tense 3sg of آوردن 'to bring'), literally 'brought before/presented.' هرچ گفت = 'whatever he said' (هرچ = 'whatever'; گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said'), so this whole segment means 'the virtuous one presented whatever he had said.'
segments.s219-g98: که introduces a reported or explanatory clause ('that'). با + noun marks accompaniment or comparison ('with/against'); کوه is 'mountain.' یارست is an older/poetic contraction of یار است ('is a companion / is a match'), so it behaves as a present-tense predication. و connects the two parallel predicates; پیل جفت uses جفت in the sense of 'a match / pair' (poetically 'equal to'), so the clause states the claim that he is a match for mountains and equal to an elephant.
segments.s219-g99: از ایرانیان نیست چندین سخن is a nominal clause meaning 'among/of the Iranians there are not many words' — از here means 'among/of,' ایرانیان is the group, نیست is the negative existential 'is not / there are not,' and چندین سخن = 'many / several words.' The word order (از + group + نیست + quantity + noun) is typical for negated existential statements in epic Persian.
segments.s219-g100: دل جنگجویان چنین بد مکن is an imperative admonition: دل جنگجویان = 'the hearts of the warriors' (possessive/genitive grouping), چنین بد = 'so (thus) bad / so distressing,' and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (make/do).' Together it commands 'do not make the warriors' hearts so sad/evil,' where مکن negates the action expressed by the preceding adjective phrase.
segments.s219-g101: بایران نمانیم یک سرفراز is jussive/imperative in tone. نمانیم is first-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive 'let us not remain' (negated by the preceding context), یک سرفراز is the direct object/goal 'one proud/victorious (man).' The initial بایران is ambiguous in form here (poetic orthography), so it is best read as part of the locution giving context or emphasis to the exhortation ('in/for Iran' or an archaic adjective); the core grammatical construction is 'نمانیم ... یک سرفراز' = 'let us not remain ... (but) let us bring forth one proud man.'
segments.s219-g102: برآریم is first-person plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us bring/raise.' گرد functions adverbially as 'around/about' or 'all around,' and از نشیب و فراز 'from low and high' (نشیب = descent/low point, فراز = ascent/height) gives the range or path of raising. The whole segment expresses the action promised in the exhortation: 'let us bring forth/raise (him) all around from low to high.'
segments.s219-g103: هرانکس که هستند is a free-relative 'whichever/ whoever there is' (هرانکس = 'whoever'), with که introducing the clause and هستند the present plural 'are/ exist.' با جاه و آب uses با 'with' to mark possession/quality ('with rank and ...'), where جاه = 'rank/status' and آب is used figuratively for 'standing / social substance / prestige.' Grammatically this segment selects which people are meant: 'whoever is with rank and standing.'
segments.s219-g104: فرستیم is first-person plural jussive/hortative 'let us send / we should send.' نزدیک before a proper name here functions adverbially/locatively 'near / to the presence of' (i.e. 'to') and names the destination افراسیاب (Afrasiab). The clause is a directive: 'let us send (them) near Afrasiab' = 'let us send them to Afrasiab.'
segments.s219-g185: سپه گفت is a simple subject+past verb: ‘the host/army said’. کین برتری خود مجوی is an admonition introduced after گفت: مجوی is a negative imperative/subjunctive (‘do not seek’), خود is a reflexive/emphatic object (‘for yourself’), برتری is the object ‘superiority/advantage’, and کین here qualifies برتری (literally ‘grudge/vengeance-superiority’) — together yielding ‘do not seek superiority prompted by this grudge’. Word order places the prohibitive verb at the end as in classical Persian.
segments.s219-g186: سخن ... مگوی is another prohibition: سخن is the direct object ‘a word/speech’, زین نشان modifies it (‘about/from this token/sign’), هیچ‌گونه is an adverbial intensifier ‘in no way/none whatsoever’, and مگوی is the negative imperative ‘do not say/utter’. The object and modifiers come before the final prohibitive verb, following Persian order.
segments.s219-g187: کزین کوه is a prepositional phrase ‘from/at this mountain’, کس is the indefinite subject ‘anyone/no one’, پیشتر is an adverb ‘further/ahead’, and نگذرد is the negative present/subjunctive third-person verb ‘does not/will not pass’. Together they state a general prohibition/statement: ‘no one will pass beyond this mountain.’
segments.s219-g188: مگر introduces the exception ‘except/unless’; رستم is the proper-name subject, این رزمگه is the object phrase ‘this battlefield’ (این = ‘this’, رزمگه = ‘battlefield’), and بنگرد is the third-person present/subjunctive of نگر ‘to look, behold’, here used concessively/conditional to yield ‘except Rostam may behold this battlefield.’
segments.s219-g189: بباشیم is a hortative/optative first-person-plural form ‘let us be/let us remain’; بر پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase ‘in the presence of/before God’ (بر + پیش + یزدان), and بپای is an exhortative verb ‘stand firm/abide’ (a present-jussive form meaning ‘be on guard’ or ‘hold fast’). The two verbs form a paired command for the group.
segments.s219-g190: که introduces the explanatory clause ‘for/that’, اویست is a third-person copular/affirmative ‘he is’ (poetic form of اوست), بر نیکوی is an intensifying phrase ‘supremely good’ or ‘very good’ (بر + نیکوی), and رهنمای is the predicate noun ‘guide’. The clause gives the reason: ‘for He is a good guide.’
segments.s219-g191: بفرمان is a prepositional phrase ‘by the command/order of’, دارنده is the agentive noun ‘the holder/possessor’, and هور و ماه is a poetic pair ‘sun and moon’ (used as an epithet for the divine power). Together they mean ‘by the command of the one who holds the sun and moon.’
segments.s219-g192: تهمتن is the proper name (Tahmātn/Rostam), بیاید is a present/subjunctive verb here used optatively/future-ish ‘may come / will come’, and بدین رزمگاه is the prepositional phrase ‘to this battlefield’ (ب + دین + رزمگاه combined meaning ‘to this’ + ‘battlefield’). The clause states that Tahmātn will come to this place by that command.
segments.s219-g33: فرامش شدم is an archaic/poetic way to render 'I forgot' or 'I became forgetful' (literally ‘I became forgetfulness’). کار آن کارزار is the direct object: کار ‘affair/concern’ + آن کارزار ‘of that battle’; word order is noun (فرامش) + auxiliary/lexical verb شدم + object phrase.
segments.s219-g34: کنونست is the contracted poetic form of کنون است ‘it is now’ and introduces two nominal predicates in parallel. The clause literally says 'now it is r͟azm (battle) and now it is kar (work)'; the repetition is emphatic/contrastive and the connective و links the two states.
segments.s219-g35: سپاهست is a fused poetic copula meaning 'there is/it is a host'; چندان is a measure word 'so much/to such an extent'. برین is the combination بر + این (here written as بر + ین) meaning 'on this/that', followed by دشت 'plain'. The phrase برین دشت و راغ lists two locations (دشت 'plain' and راغ, an obscure poetic place/term).
segments.s219-g36: که introduces a result clause 'so that / such that'. روی زمین گشت uses روی 'upon' + زمین 'ground' with گشت (perfective/become) meaning 'turned / became'. چون is a simile 'like', پر here means 'full (of)' and زاغ is 'crow/rook' — together 'became like (covered) with the feathers of crows' (i.e. swarming with crows).
segments.s219-g37: همه لشکر طوس is a genitive/possessive phrase 'all (of) the host/army of Tūs' where همه emphasizes totality. با این سپاه 'with this host' attaches as a coordinating/associative phrase ('together with this host'). Word order places the possessor/name (طوس) after لشکر as usual.
segments.s219-g38: چو is the simile marker 'as/like'. تیره 'dark' modifies the following predicate شبانست: شبانست is a poetic predicate meaning 'is night‑like / belongs to the night' (here 'is like night' or 'nightish'). با نور ماه 'with the light of the moon' completes the image — a paradoxical simile (dark night accompanied by moonlight).
segments.s219-g39: This is a list introduced by the poetic preposition ز 'from' before each place: ز چین و ز سقلاب وز هند و روم. Note that وز here is the result of the conjunctional sequence و ز (and from); in fast poetic spelling it appears as one word. The segment enumerates geographic origins (China, the obscure Ṣaqlāb, India, Rome).
segments.s219-g40: Another paired list introduced by ز 'from': ز ویران گیتی و آباد بوم. ویران گیتی is 'ruined/desolate world' and آباد بوم is 'inhabited/prosperous homeland'. The contrast (ruined vs. prosperous) is produced by the paired adjectives + nouns; word order is adjective + noun as normal.
segments.s219-g153: كه introduces an indirect question/relative clause ('that/what'), and the clause order is typical Persian: prepositional phrases ('با دیو در جنگ' = 'in/with the battle against the demon') precede the subject رستم, which itself comes before the past-tense verb كرد (کرد, 'did'). كرد is simple past, 3rd person singular. 'چه' is the interrogative 'what' (forming 'چه کرد' = 'what did ... do').
segments.s219-g154: بریشان here is the proper name 'Brishan' (subject) and is followed by the interrogative چه 'what' and the past verb آورد 'brought'. روز نبرد ('the day of battle') is a time‑phrase (روز + مضاف‌الیه نبرد) that completes the meaning. The verb آورد is simple past 3rd singular referring to what Brishan brought.
segments.s219-g155: سپاه is the subject 'the army'; آفرین is an exclamation/acclamation ('bravo'/'praise') functioning as the content shouted; خواند is past tense 3rd person singular 'uttered/sang/called'. بر پهلوان is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the champion' (بر + object). Together: 'the army cried "bravo" to the champion.'
segments.s219-g156: كه introduces reported speech/exhortation ('that'); بیدار دل is a nominal phrase 'wakeful/alert heart' where بیدار is adjective modifying دل; باش is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'be!' addressing the hero; و links the two predicates; روشن‌روان is a compound adjective 'bright‑souled/cheerful' that completes the exhortation. So the army is urging: 'be alert in heart and bright of spirit.'
segments.s219-g157: بدین is a contracted locative/directional form (به + این = 'to/for this'); مژده‌گر is 'the bringer of good news' (agent noun). دیده‌خواهی is a compound noun 'a request to be seen / a petition for an audience' and functions as the subject of رواست ('is permissible/fitting'); ست is the copula attached to روا. The clause means 'for this messenger, a request for an audience is permitted.'
segments.s219-g158: كه introduces the content clause 'that...'; این مژده is the subject 'this good news'; آرایش جان ما is a possessive construction ('the ornament/adornment of our soul/life') with آرایش as head noun and جان ما as genitive; ست is the copula 'is'. The whole clause = 'that this news is the adornment of our souls.'
segments.s219-g159: کنون 'now' sets the time; چون here functions temporally ('when' / 'as') introducing the subordinate clause. تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (Tahmatan, 'the mighty one'); بیامد is past tense 3rd person singular 'he came'; بجنگ is the contracted/prepositional phrase (به جنگ) meaning 'to battle' or 'into fight'. Together: 'Now, when Tahmatan came to battle...'.
segments.s219-g160: ندارند (they do not have) is a present/plural verb used here to state lack of capability; پا ('foot') is used idiomatically with ندارند to mean 'standing/foothold' (پا ندارند = 'have no standing/cannot hold their ground'); این سپه 'this army' is the subject; با نهنگ ('with/against the nēhang') indicates the opponent — نهنگ here is used figuratively as a huge/monstrous champion ('giant'). The clause means 'this army cannot stand against the giant.'
segments.s219-g25: سپاس از + NP is a fixed felicitation phrase meaning 'thanks to / I thank'. از here is the preposition linking سپاس to its object (literally 'because of/to'), and خورشید و ماه are two coordinated nouns 'sun and moon' governed by خداوند: 'the Lord of the sun and moon.' Word order is the poetic noun phrase 'خداوند خورشید و ماه' = 'Lord of sun and moon'.
segments.s219-g26: که introduces a content clause ('that ...'). دیدم is 1st-person singular past 'I saw'. ت is the enclitic 2nd-person object pronoun 'you' and is followed by the object marker را (here written separately as ت + را = ترا), a typical classical ordering. زنده is a predicative/adjective 'alive', and بر جایگاه is a locative phrase 'on the place/seat' (here 'on the throne'). The verb precedes its object here (دیدم ترا) — a common order in Persian, especially in verse.
segments.s219-g27: ازیشان (از + ایشان) is a prepositional phrase 'from them'. ببارید is the past tense 'rained/fell' used of blood here (poetic imagery). The noun phrase گودرز خون is inverted (name + noun) meaning 'Goudarz's blood' (genitive relationship), typical of classical word-order inversion.
segments.s219-g28: که introduces an explanatory clause. بودند is the past plural of بودن 'they were' (here auxiliary/finite 'were'). کشته is a past-participle/adjective 'killed, slain' describing the people; بخاک اندرون is a locative phrase 'on/into the ground inside' (i.e. lying dead within). Together the clause reads 'for they had been slain and lay on the ground inside.'
segments.s219-g29: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = past 'said'). بنگر is an imperative 'look' or 'see' addressing the addressee; که begins the content clause explaining what to look at. از بخت بد is an adverbial phrase 'because of ill/bad fortune' (از = 'because of/of', بخت = 'fate/fortune', بد = 'bad').
segments.s219-g30: همی is an adverb of continuity 'always/still' (classical usage). بر سرم is an idiomatic locution 'upon my head' meaning 'befalling me' (بر = on/upon, سرم = 'my head'). هر زمان = 'at every time / at every moment' (intensifying frequency). بد رسد is the verb phrase 'evil/misfortune comes' where رسد is present/past-form meaning 'befalls/occurs' (here iterative sense: 'keeps befalling').
segments.s219-g31: درین is the fused locative 'در + این' = 'in this'. جنگ = 'battle/war', so درین جنگ = 'in this battle'. پور و نبیره = 'sons and descendants/scions' (coordinated subjects). نماند is the past negative sense 'did not remain / there remained none', so the phrase means 'no son or scion remained in this battle.'
segments.s219-g32: A verbless (or verblike) tricolon: سپاه و درفش و تبیره lists items 'army, standard/banner, and [tabireh]' followed by نماند 'remained not' — i.e. 'neither army, nor banner, nor (tabireh) remained.' The repeated conjunctions and final نماند give a catalogic, emphatic negation typical of epic diction.
segments.s219-g49: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame; من is the 1st‑person pronoun. کجا introduces the interrogative 'where'. گیرم is the 1st‑person form used optatively/potentially (classical Persian subjunctive/irregular present) of گرفتن meaning 'to get/find/obtain'—here used as 'shall I find'. آرامگاه is the noun 'resting place/tomb' and is the object of گیرم.
segments.s219-g50: کجا again asks 'where'. رانم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of راندن 'to drive/lead' meaning 'where shall I drive/lead'. این qualifies the following noun as 'this'. خوار is an adjective 'despised/worthless' modifying مایه 'a portion/source/resource' of سپاه 'the host/army'. The phrase expresses 'where shall I send this despised part of the army'.
segments.s219-g51: بدو is the allative/enclitic form 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb (classical word order). گفت is the reportative verb 'said' (here marked as reported speech); گودرز is the speaker (subject). After that the quoted question follows: رستم is the addressee or subject name and چه گفت 'what did he say?' is the inner question. (The second گفت is the verbal 'said' in the question.)
segments.s219-g52: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/for' giving the reason/content. گفتار 'speech/utterance' is the object; او 'his/he' is the possessor. را is the definite/object marker attached to گفتار او. نشاید is an impersonal modal 'it is not fit/it should not be'; نهفت (from نهفتن) is a verbal form meaning 'to hide/conceal' and here follows نشاید to yield 'should not be hidden'.
segments.s219-g53: فریبرز is the speaker's name, followed by گفت 'said' (reporting verb). ای is the vocative particle 'O', introducing the direct address; جهاندیده is a compound adjective 'world‑wise/experienced'; مرد is 'man' here used as a vocative 'O experienced man' (an address to the addressee).
segments.s219-g54: تهمتن (Tahmatan, an epithet for Rostam) is the agent. نفرمود is the past form of فرمود with the negative prefix ن‑, here meaning 'did not say/command' (classical negated perfect). ما 'us' is the object and را marks it; نبرد is a noun 'battle' (object/complement). The clause therefore reads 'Tahmatan did not order us to (go into) battle.'
segments.s219-g55: بباشید is the polite/plural imperative 'be/ remain' (addressing the group). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' introducing the command. اندران is a locative 'in/among them' or 'therein' pointing to the battlefield; رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield' and is the location of the imperative.
segments.s219-g56: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'must not/should not'. شدن here means 'to happen/become' (i.e., 'it should not happen'); پیش روی سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'in front of/the fore of the army' (پیش + روی 'front/face' used classically to indicate 'ahead of'). Together: 'It must not happen that you stand before (ahead of) the host.'
segments.s219-g105: همه is the plural subject ‘everyone/all’. پای کردن (here پای + کرده) is a compound verbal idea meaning ‘to fetter / to bind by the feet’; کرده is the past participle / past form used as the finite verb. به introduces the indirect object ‘to/into the care of’, and بندگران is the recipient ‘the jailers/keepers’. Word order is poetic but literal: ‘everyone (they) fettered to the jailers.’
segments.s219-g106: وز ایشان is an archaic/genitive phrase meaning ‘their weight/force’ (وز + شان). فگنده is an older past participle of فکندن ‘to throw/strike down’ and functions as the verb ‘was/was thrown/was cast’. فراوان is an adverb/adjective ‘many / plentiful’, and سران is the affected noun ‘the chiefs / heads’. The order places the possessive/force first and then the participle and its affected noun, a flexible, poetic word order.
segments.s219-g107: بایران here functions as a prepositional phrase ‘for Iran’ (classical spelling/compaction of به ایران). نمانیم is the negative verbal form ‘remained / was left’ (poetic older form rather than modern نماند). برگ درخت is the affected noun phrase ‘a leaf of the tree’ (noun + genitival complement); the whole means ‘no leaf of the tree remained for Iran.’
segments.s219-g108: نه ... و نه ... is the standard negative correlative sequence ‘neither ... nor ...’. گاه here carries a temporal/occasional sense ‘time/occasion’. The following words شاه, تاج, تخت are a series of nouns enumerated in parallel (‘king, crown, throne’), each negated by the repeated نه; the conjunction و simply links the nouns in the catalogue.
segments.s219-g109: بخندید is the past tense ‘laughed’ (in poetry the final -ند for plural subjects is often omitted but the verb agrees with پیران ‘the elders’). و connects clauses. کرد آفرین is a verbal phrase ‘made / uttered “āfarīn”’ where کرد is the past of کردن and آفرین functions as the object/utterance ‘acclamation/bravo’. The object follows the verb here, a normal poetic order.
segments.s219-g110: بران is the prepositional/locative contraction بر + ان meaning ‘upon them / to them’ and introduces the recipients of the praise. نامداران is the plural noun ‘the renowned/illustrious ones’. و links to خاقان چین, where خاقان is the title ‘Khagan / supreme ruler’ and چین specifies ‘of China’. The whole phrase names those who received the elders’ acclamation.
segments.s219-g111: بلشکر is an adverbial/compound meaning ‘with an army / at the arrival of the host’ (classical compound). گه آمد is a temporal phrase ‘when / at the time that ... came’ (گه = occasion; آمد = past ‘came’). دلی شادمان is the noun phrase ‘a joyful heart’ (noun + adjective) which serves as the subject that ‘arrived’ in the poetic inversion.
segments.s219-g112: برفتند is the past plural verb ‘they went / departed’ (classical prefix بر- plus رفتند; poetry may show slight variation in endings). ترکان is the subject ‘the Turks’ and هم marks ‘also / too’. اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase ‘in/at that time’. The verb precedes the subject here, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s219-g177: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. رستم is the subject. بیاید is the present/subjunctive form used here with a future sense 'comes/arrives'. نکوهش کند is a verb phrase formed from the noun نکوهش + کند ('to make/reproach'), i.e. 'he will reproach'; the noun acts as the action and کند is the verb.
segments.s219-g178: مگر introduces an exception/condition here — 'unless'. کین qualifies سخن as hostile/vengeful speech. را is the definite/object marker attaching to سخن. پژوهش کند is again a nominal+کند construction meaning 'examines/looks into' (پژوهش as 'investigation', کند as the verbal auxiliary).
segments.s219-g179: که opens an explanatory/subordinate clause 'that/for'. چون is a simile 'like/as'. مرغ is the comparison image 'bird'. پیچیده is a past participle/adjective 'entangled/ensnared' and بودم is the past copula 'I was' ('I had been entangled'). بدام is a poetic form (here equivalent to به دام) meaning 'into a snare / taken captive' (this particular sense is not present in the provided sense list).
segments.s219-g180: همه کار is 'all (my) deeds/affairs' with همه as quantifier. ناکام is a post‑nominal adjective 'unsuccessful/failed' modifying کار. و is the coordinating 'and'. پیکار خام: پیکار 'battle/fighting' is qualified by خام — literally 'raw/unripe' and here meaning 'ill‑timed/ill‑conceived' or 'fruitless'.
segments.s219-g181: سپهبد همان بود is a simple copular clause 'the spahbed was the same' (سپهبد subject, همان predicate, بود past copula). و لشکر همان repeats the idea elliptically: 'and the army (was) the same' with the verb omitted by ellipsis.
segments.s219-g182: کسی را ندیدم is a negative past clause 'I did not see anyone', with را marking کسی as the definite/affected object. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گردان دمان is a genitive/compound: گردان 'troop/unit' and دمان here indicates the location 'of the dāman' (in context: 'of the foothill'/'of the skirts'), so 'from the troop at the foothill'.
segments.s219-g183: یکی in this hortatory context functions adverbially 'once/one (single)'. حمله آریم is a hortative/1st‑person plural proposal 'let us make/launch an attack' — آریم here is a verbal form expressing 'let us ...' (the provided lemma senses do not match this verbal use). چون شیر نر is a simile 'like a male lion' (چون = like, شیر = lion, نر = male).
segments.s219-g184: شوند is 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/optative 'they will be / they may become' used to express the expected result. از بن is an adverbial phrase 'from the root' = 'utterly/completely' (i.e. root and branch). که here links the result clause, and مگر introduces an exception 'unless'; زاستر is a proper name referenced as the possible exception or intervening factor.
segments.s219-g81: همانا is an emphatic opening particle ('truly'). به here functions instrumentally ('by/with') before the noun phrase. تنها is a prepositive adjective 'single/alone' modifying تن 'person/self'; the possessive من follows the noun (a common classical order: noun + possessor). نیند is an archaic verbal element here forming part of a prohibition ('do not ...'); it stands after the noun phrase and introduces the negative command clause.
segments.s219-g82: نگویی is a negative imperative/subjunctive 2nd-person form 'do not say' (ن + گویی), here followed by the complementizer که 'that'. ایرانیان is the plural subject 'the Iranians'. خود is an emphatic/reflexive marker 'themselves/own'. کیند is an archaic 3rd-person plural present form equivalent to modern کنند 'they do' and completes the subordinate clause.
segments.s219-g83: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. ترسانی here functions as a predicative adjective/participial form meaning 'frightened/afraid' (the copula 'هستی' is omitted, as is common in poetry). از introduces the cause/agent 'of/by' and links to the name رستم, which is followed by the postpositive adjective نامدار 'renowned'.
segments.s219-g84: نخستین is an adverb/adv phrase 'first, at the beginning'. ازو is the prepositional phrase 'from him / upon him' (classical clitic form). من is the 1st-person pronoun 'I' (subject). برآرم is a 1st-singular present/stative form with a volitional/near-future force 'I lift up / I bring about' (classical usage often renders this as 'I will [do X]'). دمار is the object 'ruin/destruction' and completes the verb phrase 'I will bring ruin'.
segments.s219-g85: گرش here is the conditional particle گر ('if') merged with the clitic pronoun ش ('him') — in classical verse these often appear together as one token. یک زمان is an adverbial phrase 'for one moment / once / ever'. اندر is a preposition 'in/into'. آرم in this context functions as a 1st-person verb 'I place / I put' (classical forms vary), and بدا م here reads as به دام 'into the snare/trap' (written together); the whole segment expresses the conditional 'if I should put him into a trap even for a moment'.
segments.s219-g86: نمانم is a negative optative/subjunctive first-person form 'may I not remain' or idiomatically 'I will not permit / I will not be such that ...' introducing a strong negation of allowance. که is the complementizer 'that'. ماند is the (subjunctive/present) verb 'remain/last'. بگیتیش is a compound locative 'in his world' (ب + گیتی + ش). نام is 'name' here meaning 'reputation/fame' — the clause says he will not let the other's name remain in the world.
segments.s219-g87: تو is 'you'. از introduces the source/cause 'by/from' (here 'weary of'). لشکر سیستان is the noun phrase 'the army of Sistan' (لشکر 'army' + place name). خسته‌ is the predicate adjective 'weary/tired'; the following ای (orthographically attached) marks the 2nd-person copula (you are) in poetic speech, so the whole means 'you are weary of the Sistan army.'
segments.s219-g88: دل خویش = 'your heart' (دل 'heart' + خوی + ش forming the possessive 'own/your'). در introduces the locative/oblique 'in/at', and جنگشان is 'their war/fighting' (جنگ + شان 'their'). بسته‌ای is a perfect/participial form meaning 'bound/closed' — metaphorically 'you have bound/committed your heart' to the fight (a common Persian idiom: دل را بستن بهِ something = to attach one's heart to something).
segments.s219-g41: همانا is an emphatic adverb ('truly/behold'). نماندست is a poetic, fused past-negative ('نماند' + enclitic/auxiliary) meaning 'did not remain' (verb before the noun by inversion). یک جانور is the noun phrase ('a single beast/animal') placed after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s219-g42: مگر introduces the exception ('except'). بسته is a past-participle/adjectival participle here meaning 'bound/tied'. The prepositional phrases بر جنگِ ما بر کمر specify location: 'on our war-(belt/gear) at the waist' — classical/poetic word order repeats بر for two closely linked locatives.
segments.s219-g43: کنون = 'now'. تا introduces a temporal/subordinate clause here meaning 'until'. نگویی is a classical subjunctive form of گفتن used after تا to mean 'you tell'/'you say' (i.e. 'until you say/tell'); this is an older/poetic verb form. که رستم کجاست is the embedded interrogative clause ('that/where Rostam is') with the copula ست following the wh-word (کجاست = 'is where').
segments.s219-g44: ز = 'from/because of' (poetic). غمها = plural 'sorrows/griefs'. نگردد is an impersonal/negative-present form of گردیدن here meaning 'does not become/turn' (i.e. 'will not become straight'). م + را in the segmentation represent the enclitic accusative 'mara' = 'me' ('م' mis-annotated here) plus the object marker 'را'; پشت راست = 'straight back' (literally 'back straight'), the predicate affected by the grief. The clause means 'because of griefs my back will not straighten.'
segments.s219-g45: فریبرز گفت is 'Fariburz said' with گفت as the past-tense verb 'said' (simple past). از پسِ من = 'from behind me' or 'after me' (پس = 'after/behind') and ز جای = 'from his place' — both phrases give the origin of the person mentioned; word order is compact and somewhat inverted in classical style.
segments.s219-g46: بیامد is past tense 'came'. نبودش is the negative copula plus enclitic ('there was not of him' / 'he had not') used to mean 'he had nothing' or 'there was nothing with him'. جز = 'except'. از رزم رای = 'except from/of battle-plan/counsel' where رای means 'plan/counsel' — so the clause says his only intention/possession was a plan for battle.
segments.s219-g47: شب تیره = 'the dark night' (adjective after noun in poetic compression). را marks the span or topicalizes the time-phrase. تا introduces the time limit 'until' and سپیده‌دمان is a poetic compound meaning 'dawn / the time of first light' (سپیده + دمان). Together: 'the dark night until the break of dawn.'
segments.s219-g48: بیاید is a 3rd-person subjunctive/present used with تا ('until ... comes') meaning 'comes' or 'may come'. بره is the noun 'lamb' (the subject in this clause) placed after the verb by poetic inversion. بر + نجوید زمان = 'does not seek/ask for the time' (نجوید is present/negative form 'does not seek'); overall the line reads as a short, somewhat elliptical statement: 'until dawn comes, the lamb does not seek the time.'
